mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 5 Series engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned BMW 5 Series engine typically costs £2,800–£6,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], with the volume N47/B47 diesels at £2,800–£5,500 and the B58 petrol at £3,800–£6,000. V8 engines (N63/S63) cost significantly more — £6,000–£14,000. Fitting adds £650–£2,100. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 5 Series engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B58B30 straight-six in the G30 540i — the most dependable modern BMW engine in our dataset. From the classic era, the M57D30 in the E39/E60 530d is one of the most durable diesel engines ever built, regularly exceeding 250,000 miles. Both share one thing: regular oil changes are non-negotiable.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 5 Series engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but a pre-2016 N47D20C without a documented timing-chain replacement is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The N54B30's HPFP issues are also costly — check history before buying any E60 535i. A 2016+ B47/B48/B58 has no such caveat.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my 5 Series engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: By engine: cold-start rattle (N47), long cranking and limp mode (N54 HPFP), valve seal smoke on startup (N63). On the M54/M57, cooling system failure is the main cause of engine death — watch for coolant loss. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £2,800–£6,000 engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 5 Series engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends entirely on generation. Against a £35,000 G30, a £5,000 B58 recon is just 14% of the car's value — ✅ safe. Against a £22,000 F10, a £4,500 N47 recon is 20% — ✅ safe. Against a £4,500 E60, a £4,000 N47 recon is 89% — ❌ walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 5 Series generation should I buy?

acceptedAnswer.text: 2017+ G30 540i with the B58 — the modern straight-six in a depreciated executive chassis, no chain caveat, 335 bhp, and the sharpest 5 Series chassis ever. For the classic bargain, E39 530d with the M57 — the engine is bulletproof, the values are still sensible. For diesel economy, G30 520d with B47. Avoid early N47/N54 cars without documented history.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 5 Series engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation, model year and variant (520d, 530d, 540i) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: BMW 5 Series vs 3 Series — which is more reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Mechanically they share engines across the same eras — the N47/B47/B58 story is identical. The 5 Series is heavier, so it works the powertrain harder; the 3 Series is lighter but benefits from the same engineering. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The 5 Series offers more space and comfort; the 3 Series offers more agility.

hero.tagPill: BMW 5 Series • 1972–Present • 8 Generations (E12, E28, E34, E39, E60, F10, G30, G60) • M57, N47, N52, N54, N55, B47, B48, B58, N63
hero.h1: 5 Series Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every BMW 5 Series engine across eight generations — from the carburetted sixes of the E12 to the B58 straight-six and electrified powertrains of the G60. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for the executive saloon that defined a segment. Backed by 500+ real UK 5 Series engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The 5 Series rule — generation is destiny: an N47 recon at £3,200–£5,500 against a £6,000 E60 is 53–92% of the car's value; the same engine against a £22,000 F10 is 15–25%. Against a £35,000 G30, a B58 recon at £3,800–£6,000 is just 11–17%. Run the maths per generation.
★★★★☆ — one badge, eight distinct engineering eras. The M57 diesel and B58 petrol are amongst BMW's finest; the N47 chain question and N54 HPFP issues defined their eras.
B58B30 (G30 540i) and M57D30 (E60/E39 530d) — the modern and classic straight-six gold standards
Pre-2013 N47D20C (E60/F10 520d) without documented chain replacement • N54B30 (E60 535i) with HPFP issues
2017+ G30 540i with B58 — the modern straight-six in a depreciated executive chassis, no chain caveat
2016+ F10 LCI / G30 with B47 or B57 — revised architecture, strong economy
N47D20C timing chain / N54 HPFP — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The 5 Series is BMW's executive backbone — the car that defined the premium saloon for half a century. The M57 diesel is one of the most dependable engines ever made; the N47 chain question and N54 HPFP issues are the only clouds over the modern era. Buy on the engine, not the badge.

generations.h2: BMW 5 Series Generations — Find Your Vehicle
generations.subHeadline: Eight generations, fifty-plus years, and one nameplate that defined the executive saloon. The right 5 Series depends entirely on which era you're willing to live with — and which engine.

cards.title: BMW 5 Series E12
cards.verdict: The original — the car that created the executive saloon segment. Now a genuine classic where preservation, not daily-driver economics, drives the decision. M30 six-cylinder cars are the ones to seek.

cards.title: BMW 5 Series E28
cards.verdict: The E28 refined the formula — sharper handling, better engines, and the M535i that defined the sports saloon. M30 sixes and the M21 diesel are the robust choices; bodywork is the real project.

cards.title: BMW 5 Series E34
cards.verdict: The E34 is the 5 Series at its most beautifully engineered — the M50 six-cylinder is near-bulletproof, the M60 V8 is smooth but carries Nikasil liner caveats on early cars. A rising classic.

cards.title: BMW 5 Series E39
cards.verdict: The 5 Series at its peak — often called the best executive saloon ever made. The M57 diesel (530d) is one of the most dependable engines BMW ever built; the M54 petrol is smooth and robust. Cooling systems are the only real caveat.

cards.title: BMW 5 Series E60/E61
cards.verdict: The controversial Bangle-era 5 Series — advanced for its time, but the N47 diesel's rear-chain tensioner and the N54's HPFP issues define this generation's reputation. The M57 530d is the robust pick; the N52 petrol six is the smooth one.

cards.title: BMW 5 Series F10/F11
cards.verdict: The generation that returned to classic proportions — and quietly fixed some of the E60's issues. Early N47 diesels carry the chain caveat; 2016+ B47 cars are the sweet spot. The 535i with N55 is the petrol pick; the M5 with S63 is the full-fat experience.

cards.title: BMW 5 Series G30/G31
cards.verdict: The CLAR-platform 5 Series — lighter, sharper, and the most dependable modern 5 Series ever made. B-family engines only (except the V8), so the chain caveat is gone. The 540i with the B58 is the sweet spot; the 530d with B57 is the diesel perfection.

cards.title: BMW 5 Series G60/G61
cards.meta: 2023–present • B48, B58, B57, plus i5 electric
cards.verdict: The eighth-generation 5 Series — available with combustion, mild-hybrid, and fully electric i5 powertrains. The B58 in the 540i is as robust as ever; the i5 is the future. Too new for volume reliability data, but the B-family record is impeccable.

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 5 Series Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine ever fitted to a BMW 5 Series — verified codes, specifications and real UK ownership data.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: M57D30, N47D20C, B58B30 and B47D20A ratings: 95% confidence — high-volume engines with deep specialist rebuild history and clear failure patterns from BMW TSB data and 100+ specialist interviews. N55B30, N63B44 and B57D30 ratings: 90% confidence — well-documented patterns from the F10/G30 eras. E12/E28/E34/E39 engines: Expert-knowledge tier — decades of documented patterns and enthusiast records, not proprietary volume data. Data note (internal) — corrections applied: Correction applied: The source data listed "N47D20" as a single engine. Verified reality is that the E60 received the N47D20C (2007–2010, 163–177 bhp), while the F10 LCI (2016+) received the B47D20A. The N47D20C is the high-risk chain-tensioner engine; the B47D20A is the revised front-chain architecture. Correction applied: The N54B30 HPFP issue affects the E60 535i (2007–2010) — this is the defining failure of that engine, not a timing chain problem. Updated the Known Failure Patterns accordingly. Correction applied: The M57 diesel in the E39/E60 is one of the most dependable engines BMW ever made — the 530d (M57D30) regularly exceeds 250,000 miles with proper maintenance. This is the engine to seek in older 5 Series cars. Correction applied: The N63B44 "hot-vee" V8 (F10/G30 550i/M550i) has well-documented heat-related failures — valve stem seals, turbo coolant lines, and battery drain. The S63 in the M5 is more robust but carries M-car servicing costs.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The six most common failure patterns across eight generations — symptoms, costs and urgency.

problems.description: The defining 5 Series diesel issue — affects E60 and pre-2016 F10 520d. Cold-start rattle from the rear of the engine is the warning; ignored, chain slip means a full engine replacement. £3,200–£5,500 recon [BMW-QUOTE].

The twin-turbo 535i's Achilles heel — high-pressure fuel pump failure leads to long cranks and limp mode; injector issues follow. Affects E60 535i (2007–2010). BMW extended warranty covered many; check history before buying.

The N63's defining problems — valve stem seals, turbo coolant lines, and battery drain from the hot-vee design. Affects F10 550i and early G30 550i/M550i. High ownership cost; known as the "N63 nightmare" in specialist circles.

The E39/E60 era's most common failure — plastic water pump impellers, expansion tanks and thermostat housings all degrade. Overheating kills the alloy heads. Replace proactively every 60k miles on M54 petrols; M57 diesels are more robust but not immune.
Not an engine failure — but a common non-engine 5 Series enquiry. Front thrust arm bushings wear on E39/E60, causing shimmy under braking. Rear subframe mounts also fatigue on high-mileage cars.
The modern B-family engines' known weak points — coolant expansion tank leaks and water pump failures. Affects G30 540i/530i (2017+). £400–£800 repair, not catastrophic if caught early.

marketIntelligence.h2: BMW 5 Series Market Intelligence — What 500+ UK Owners Told Us in 2025

signals.demandTrend: ⬆ Rising — the N47 chain question continues to drive demand [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
➡ Stable — newer cars generate due-diligence searches [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]

insights: Interesting insight: The 5 Series generates the third-highest BMW enquiry volume in our dataset — but the split tells the story: the F10 (300) dominates, followed by the E60 (226), with the G30 (177) showing that newer cars generate due-diligence searches, not failure enquiries. The N47's chain question and the N54's HPFP issues are still driving demand years after production ended. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)

E60 enquiries are concentrated on the 2007–2010 diesel and 535i petrol parc now exiting affordable-warranty age — exactly the cars where a £3,200–£6,500 engine decision meets a £4,000–£8,000 vehicle. The 70% threshold is live on this generation; on a tired E60 N47, we'll honestly tell you to walk away. (Editorial synthesis.)

G30 enquiries are dominated by extended-warranty and due-diligence questions, not failures. Owners are nervous about the N63 and N47 headlines from previous generations — but the B-family engines are proving the most dependable 5 Series powertrains ever made. The 540i with B58 is the G30 to buy. (Editorial synthesis.)

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW 5 Series Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you buy one 5 Series based on this page, buy a 2017+ G30 540i with the B58 — the modern straight-six is the most dependable BMW engine in decades, and the G30 chassis is the sharpest 5 Series ever made. If the budget doesn't stretch, an E39 530d with the M57 is the classic bargain — but on an E60 or early F10 N47, a documented timing-chain inspection is worth more than any service stamp.

replacementCosts.h2: BMW 5 Series Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for used, reconditioned and rebuilt engines — supply only, with labour guidance.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a BMW 5 Series engine typically takes 8–22 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE]. The 5 Series rule — generation is destiny: A £3,200–£5,500 N47 recon against a £6,000 E60 sits at 53–92% of the car's value — on a sub-£5k E60, that exceeds our 70% BMW threshold, and we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a £22,000 F10, the same recon is 15–25% — ✅ safe. Against a £35,000 G30, a £3,800–£6,000 B58 recon is just 11–17% — a routine repair. Value the car first, then the engine. The M57 in the E39 is the classic bargain — robust enough to justify a £3,000–£4,500 recon on a £6,000 car.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a BMW 5 Series engine typically takes 8–22 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE]. The 5 Series rule — generation is destiny: A £3,200–£5,500 N47 recon against a £6,000 E60 sits at 53–92% of the car's value — on a sub-£5k E60, that exceeds our 70% BMW threshold, and we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a £22,000 F10, the same recon is 15–25% — ✅ safe. Against a £35,000 G30, a £3,800–£6,000 B58 recon is just 11–17% — a routine repair. Value the car first, then the engine. The M57 in the E39 is the classic bargain — robust enough to justify a £3,000–£4,500 recon on a £6,000 car.

engineEvolution.h2: How the BMW 5 Series Engine Range Evolved — 1972 to Today

eras.whyBmwChanged: The 5 Series launched with the M10 four-cylinder and M30 six-cylinder from the E12; the M21 diesel arrived in 1983 as the first BMW diesel.
eras.worthKnowing: The M30 six-cylinder is one of BMW's most durable engines — 200k+ miles is routine. The M21 diesel is robust but slow by modern standards.

eras.whyBmwChanged: BMW introduced the M60 V8 for the E34 530i/540i — the first V8 in a 5 Series. The M50 six-cylinder replaced the M20; the M51 diesel replaced the M21.
eras.worthKnowing: The M60 V8's Nikasil liners suffered from high-sulphur fuel issues on early cars — buy with a liner test or post-1995 build. The M50 is near-bulletproof.

eras.keyEngines: M52, M54, M57 diesel, M62 V8
eras.whyBmwChanged: The E39 is considered the best 5 Series ever made — and the M57 diesel (530d) is one of the most dependable engines BMW ever built.
eras.worthKnowing: The M57 regularly exceeds 250,000 miles with proper maintenance — this is the engine to seek. The M62 V8 is smooth but the timing chain guides are the caveat.

eras.whyBmwChanged: Emissions targets forced the move to direct injection and turbos. The N47 was BMW's first modern common-rail diesel; the N54 brought twin turbos to the 535i.
eras.worthKnowing: The N47's rear-chain tensioner and the N54's HPFP issues defined this era. The M57 530d is the robust pick; the N52 six-cylinder is the smooth petrol.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The F10 returned to classic proportions and introduced the ActiveHybrid 5. The LCI (2016) quietly replaced the N47 with the B47 — the reliability story changed more than the styling.
eras.worthKnowing: The B47 finally fixed the chain issue that defined the N47. The N55 is robust; the N63 V8 carries the "hot-vee" heat issues. The LCI B47 cars are the F10 to buy.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The CLAR-platform G30 brought B-family engines, mild-hybrid tech and the 530e plug-in hybrid. The 540i with B58 is the modern sweet spot.
eras.worthKnowing: The B-family engines are the most dependable 5 Series powertrains ever made — no chain caveat, robust cooling systems. The 540i is the G30 to buy.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The eighth-generation 5 Series — available with combustion, mild-hybrid, and fully electric i5 powertrains. The i5 is the future; the B58 is the combustion gold standard.
eras.worthKnowing: The i5 is too new for volume reliability data, but the B-family record is impeccable. The G60 540i with B58 is the safe combustion choice.

whoShouldBuy.h2: Who Should Buy a BMW 5 Series?

profiles.verdict: The 5 Series Touring (E61/F11/G31) is one of the most capable family estates on sale — room for five, 500+ litres of boot, and a B57 diesel that returns 50+ mpg on the motorway. Buy 2017+ G31 530d for the modern sweet spot; an E39 530d Touring for the classic bargain.

This is what the 5 Series does best — 50 miles a day on the motorway is the 530d's natural habitat. The G30 520d with B47 returns 60+ mpg; the G30 530i with B48 is the petrol choice. The F10 and G30 are commuting perfection.

A cheap E60 N47 is not a cheap 5 Series — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £8,000 E60 530d with the M57 is the bargain; a £5,000 520d without chain paperwork is the trap. The E39 530d is the classic value proposition.

The G30 540i with the B58 is the modern sweet spot — 335 bhp, rear-wheel-drive, and the most dependable modern BMW straight-six. The M5 (F10/G30) is the full-fat experience, but the 540i offers 80% of the performance for half the running costs.

An E39 530d with the M57 or a G30 540i with the B58 are the 5 Series keepers — engines that will outlast the cars around them. The E39 is the rising classic; the G30 is the modern keeper. Buy the best example you can afford; maintenance is an investment, not an expense.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My BMW 5 Series Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: Cold-start rattle on an N47? HPFP issues on an N54? On the 5 Series, the answer swings on your car's value — an E60 at £6,000 and a G30 at £35,000 face very different economics. Get a personalised repair-vs-replace verdict using real UK cost data.
